sections.heading: Shard a Global Collection for Global Writes in the Atlas UI
sections.body:
> **Note:** 
>
> - Global writes are only supported on global clusters, which
>   require an `M30+` cluster tier.
>
> - When Global Writes are enabled, Global Clusters do not support dedicated search nodes.
>
> - You can use the Atlas UI to shard a global collection only
>   for global clusters with [Atlas-Managed Sharding](atlas/tutorial/create-global-cluster)
>   enabled.
>
> - This document assumes prior knowledge of sharding semantics.
>   Review the [Sharding documentation](manual/sharding) before
>   continuing with this tutorial.

In sharded clusters, you can create [zones](manual/reference/glossary) of sharded
data based on the [shard keys](manual/reference/glossary). Your zones can segment data
based on geographic area. To learn more about sharding based
on geographic area, see [Segmenting Data by Location](manual/tutorial/sharding-segmenting-data-by-location).

The Atlas UI supports sharding Global Write collections
for only global clusters that use [Atlas-Managed Sharding](atlas/tutorial/create-global-cluster).
To shard non-Global-Write collections or Global Write collections
that use Self-Managed Sharding, you must use [mongosh](mongodb-shell) or application
code. To learn more, see [Deploy a Sharded Cluster](manual/tutorial/deploy-shard-cluster).

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
You must have the [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)  or [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) role for the cluster's Atlas project to shard a
collection for Global Writes in the Atlas UI.

sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
Before starting this procedure, review:

- [global write](global-writes-collections) for a Global Cluster.

- [Atlas Limits](atlas-limitations) for Global Cluster shard keys.

You can configure [Online Archives](online-archive-overview) to
move infrequently accessed data from your Atlas cluster to a
MongoDB-managed read-only federated database instance instead of sharding your collection
or upgrading your cluster tier. To learn more about Online Archive, see
[Manage Online Archives](manage-online-archive).

After selecting a shard key via the Atlas UI, Atlas attempts to
shard the target collection. During this period, ensure that no user
manually shards the collection. If Atlas detects that the target
collection was sharded with a different key than the one selected via
the Atlas UI, Atlas stops the procedure and displays a warning
for that collection in the Atlas UI. You can click
**Unmanage Collection** to clear the warning. This action
removes the collection from Atlas management while leaving the
underlying collection and the manually chosen shard key intact.

sections.heading: Update the Shard Key Value
sections.body:
You can update a document's shard key value
unless the shard key field is the immutable `_id` field. To learn
more, see [Change a Document's Shard Key Value](manual/core/sharding-shard-key).

In MongoDB earlier to 6.0, you can't update the value of a shard key
field in an existing document in a Global Writes-enabled collection.

> **Important:** 
>
> Shard Key Values and Global Clusters
>
> In all MongoDB versions, you can't [reshard a collection](manual/core/sharding-reshard-a-collection) on a Global Cluster.
> To learn more, see [global write](global-writes-collections).

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To shard a collection in a Global Cluster:

You can choose one of the following options:

| Default                           | Atlas uses the document that specifies the field or fields as the shard key.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Use unique index as the shard key | Atlas uses the underlying index to enforce a unique constraint on the shard key of the Global Collection.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| Use hashed index as the shard key | Atlas distributes the sharded data evenly by hashing the second field of the shard key.You can optionally select Pre-split data for even distribution to specify whether to perform initial chunk creation and distribution for an empty or non-existing collection based on the defined zones and zone ranges for the collection.If you select the Pre-split data for even distribution option, you can also specify the minimum number of chunks to create initially when sharding an empty collection with a hashed shard key. Initial chunk distribution allows Atlas to setup zoned sharding quickly. The number of chunks the Atlas creates depends on the number of zones that you define. By default, Atlas creates one chunk per location code and distributes chunks evenly across all shards. |

To learn more about these options, see
[Global Clusters Sharding Reference](global-cluster-sharding).

Atlas displays the compound shard key near the top of the
**Global Writes** tab after configuring the collection.

sections.heading: Global Cluster Sharding Reference
sections.body:
The following sections describe [sharding](manual/sharding)
behavior and how to enable sharding for [Manage Global Clusters](global-clusters).

sections.heading: Sharding Collections for Global Writes
sections.body:
For global clusters with [Atlas-Managed Sharding](atlas/tutorial/create-global-cluster)
enabled, unsharded collections must meet the following compatibility
requirements *prior to sharding* to use Global Writes when
sharded:

- Every document in the collection **must** include a `location`
  field.

- The value of the `location` field **must** be either an
  ISO-3166-1 alpha 2 country code (`"US"`, `"DE"`, `"IN"`) *or* a
  supported ISO-3166-2 subdivision code (`"US-DC"`, `"DE-BE"`,
  `"IN-DL"`). Documents that don't match this criteria can't be routed
  to any shard in the cluster. To view the complete list of
  currently supported country or subdivision codes, see
  [https://cloud.mongodb.com/static/atlas/country\_iso\_codes.txt](https://cloud.mongodb.com/static/atlas/country_iso_codes.txt).

A shard key on the `location` field alone might result in bottlenecks,
especially for workloads where a subset of countries or subdivisions
receive the majority of write operations. Atlas Global Writes
requires a [compound shard key](manual/core/index-compound) to
facilitate the efficient distribution of sharded data across the
cluster. Atlas Global Cluster shard keys share the same
restrictions as MongoDB shard keys. The following Atlas
Global Cluster limitations apply:

- The first field of the compound shard key must be `location` and
  *can't* be hashed.

- There can only be one secondary shard key field in a compound shard
  key.

- The secondary shard key field of a compound shard key *can* be hashed.

- The secondary shard key field *can't* be an array.

To learn more about:

- Choosing a secondary shard key field and the effect of shard key choices
  on data distribution, see [Choosing a Shard Key](manual/core/sharding-choose-a-shard-key).

- Shard key limitations, see [Shard Key Limitations](manual/reference/limits).

- Available hashed sharding options, see [Hashed Sharding Options](global-writes-initial-chunks).

> **Important:** 
>
> Changing the Shard Key
>
> After sharding, what you can modify depends on the version of
> MongoDB that you run:
>
> | MongoDB Version | Modify Shard Key Keys                             | Modify Shard Key Values |
> | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- |
> | MongoDB 7.0     | Yes, add fields to a key using the Atlas UI only. | Yes                     |
> | MongoDB 8.0     | Yes, add fields to a key using the Atlas UI only. | Yes                     |
>
> In all MongoDB versions, you can't [reshard a collection](manual/core/sharding-reshard-a-collection) on a Global Cluster.

The [Atlas UI](atlas-ui) supports creating sharded
collections with specific validations for Global Writes.
To learn more, see [Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes).

You can also use [mongosh](mongodb-shell) to execute the
[sh.shardCollection()](manual/reference/method/sh.shardCollection). After sharding
the collection, you must use the [Atlas UI](atlas-ui)
to enable Global Writes for that collection.

WARNING: If you shard a collection that already has a MongoDB Search
index, you might experience a brief period of incomplete search results
while the index is building on a shard. Also, if you add a shard for
an already sharded collection that contains a MongoDB Search index, your search
queries against that collection might return incomplete results until
the initial sync process completes on the added shards. To learn more,
see [initial sync process](troubleshoot-initial-sync).

To learn more about sharding collections via the Atlas UI, see
[Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes).

sections.heading: Hashed Sharding Options
sections.body:
Shard keys use [hashed sharding](manual/core/hashed-sharding) and
pre-split data for even distribution.

Atlas distributes the sharded data evenly by hashing the second
field of the shard key if you perform one of the following actions:

- Enable the use of the hashed index shard keys by selecting
  **Use hashed index as the shard key** in the
  [Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes).

- Set `isCustomShardKeyHashed` through the [API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupclusterglobalwritemanagednamespace).

You can optionally specify whether to perform initial chunk creation and
distribution for an empty or non-existing collection. This action is
based on the defined zones and zone ranges for the collection. To do
this, perform one of the following actions:

- Select **Pre-split data for even distribution**
  in the [Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes).

- Set `presplitHashedZone` using the [API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupclusterglobalwritemanagednamespace).

When you create a sharded collection using a compound hashed shard key
for Global Clusters, Atlas creates at least 1 chunk per
location [code](https://cloud.mongodb.com/static/atlas/country_iso_codes.txt)
and attempts to distribute chunks evenly across shards in the
cluster.

You can also specify the minimum number of chunks to create initially
when sharding an empty collection with a [hashed shard key](manual/core/hashed-sharding) using the [Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes) or by
setting the `numInitialChunks` parameter through the
[API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupclusterglobalwritemanagednamespace).

> **Note:** If you specify the number of chunks per shard, Atlas creates at
> least the minimum number of chunks that you specified, with the same
> number of chunks per location code. If you specify the minimum
> number of chunks, Atlas sets up zoned sharding quickly,
> especially if you already know how to geographically distribute your
> data before sharding.

sections.heading: Error Handling
sections.body:
If Atlas encounters an error while sharding a collection for global
writes, a message appears in the banner at the top of the screen.* Click **See Details** to learn about the error and the
  namespace where the error occurred. A modal window appears with the
  complete error message and a **Fix Now** button.

* Click **Close** and navigate to the collection in the
  Atlas UI. You can also click the **Fix Now** button to
  go to the Atlas UI for that Atlas cluster.

* Click the **Global Writes** tab for the collection mentioned
  in the error message.

* Click **Unmanage Collection** to cancel the global writes
  sharding operation. You must have the
  [Project Data Access Admin](<Project Data Access Admin>) role to cancel the sharding
  operation.After you make any necessary changes to the collection as indicated by
the error message, you can start the sharding process again.Possible errors include:An index already exists on the custom shard key.If the field chosen as the second part of the compound shard key is
already indexed, the sharding operation may fail.The collection is already sharded.If the collection has already been manually sharded, the operation
fails.The collection has a custom default collation.A [custom default collation](manual/reference/collation) on the
collection may cause a sharding error.

sections.heading: Global Cluster Write Operations
sections.body:
For each document in a write operation, MongoDB
uses the `location` field of the shard key (if included) to determine
the zone to route the data to. MongoDB selects a shard associated to
that zone as the target for writing the document, facilitating
geographically isolated and segmented data storage.

> **Warning:** If a shard key isn't included in the write operation,
> or a shard key is included, but the `location` field isn't present,
> the write operation will succeed, but the resulting documents will
> not be distributed.

MongoDB can guarantee this behavior only for inserted documents that
meet the criteria defined in [global write](global-writes-collections).
Specifically, MongoDB can route a document whose `location` field
doesn't conform to ISO-3166-1 alpha 2 *or* ISO-3166-2 to any shard in the
cluster.

sections.heading: Global Cluster Read Operations
sections.body:
MongoDB query routing depends on whether the read operation includes
the full shard key **and** that the `location` value corresponds to a
supported ISO-3166-1 alpha 2 country code (`"US"`, `"DE"`, `"IN"`)
*or* a supported ISO-3166-2 subdivision code (`"US-DC"`,
`"DE-BE"`, `"IN-DL"`).

- For queries that do include the full shard key and whose `location`
  value meets the requirements for Global Writes, MongoDB targets the
  read operation to the zone that maps to the `location` value or
  values specified in the query.

- For read operations that don't include the `location` value , or if
  the `location` value doesn't correspond to a supported
  ISO-3166-1 alpha 2 country code or ISO-3166-2 subdivision code, MongoDB
  must broadcast the read operation to every zone in the cluster.

- For Global Writes zones that have **Read-only** nodes in
  geographically distant regions, clients in those regions can query
  the local **Read-only** node for that zone by specifying the
  full shard key as part of the query *and* issuing the read
  operation with a [Read Preference](manual/core/read-preference)
  of [nearest](manual/core/read-preference).

> **Important:** Secondary reads might return stale data depending on the level
> of replication lag between the secondary node and the primary.

To learn more, see [MongoDB Read Preference](manual/core/read-preference)
and [MongoDB Query Routing](manual/core/sharded-cluster-query-router).

sections.heading: Sharding Collections without Global Writes
sections.body:
Global Writes clusters support the same
[Ranged](manual/core/ranged-sharding) and
[Hashed](manual/core/hashed-sharding) sharding strategies
as a standard Atlas sharded cluster. For sharded collections
whose shard keys and document schema don't support Global Writes,
MongoDB distributes the sharded data evenly across the available shards
in the cluster with respect to the chosen
[shard key](manual/core/sharding-shard-key). Consider using a
separate sharded cluster for data that can't take advantage of
Global Writes.

You can't modify a collection to support Global Writes after
sharding. We recommend that you choose a shard key that will
allow you to use Global Writes for a collection in the future.

> **See Also:** To learn more about Global Writes sharding requirements, see
> [global write](global-writes-collections).

sections.heading: Unsharded Collections in Global Write Clusters
sections.body:
Global Clusters provide the same support for unsharded
collections as a standard Atlas sharded cluster. For each
database in the cluster, MongoDB stores its unsharded collections
on a [primary shard](manual/reference/glossary). Use [sh.status()](manual/reference/method/sh.status) from
[mongosh](mongodb-shell) to determine the primary shard for the database.
